g.g: Definition and Overview <text x="10" y="25" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#2f4f7f" text-anchor="start" data-narration="A repeated game is a game played multiple times over a finite horizon, where each player makes decisions based on the payoffs from previous periods. The main idea is to analyze the behavior of players when they have multiple opportunities to make decisions and can adjust their strategies accordingly."

Types of Repeated Games <text x="10" y="25" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#2f4f7f" text-anchor="start" data-narration="Zero-Sum Repeated Games are games where one player's gain is equal to the other player's loss. Examples include prisoner's dilemma and hawk-dove games. Non-Zero-Sum Repeated Games have payoffs that are not necessarily zero-sum. Examples include auctions and bargaining games. Finite Horizon Repeated Games have a fixed number of periods, while Infinite Horizon Repeated Games continue indefinitely."

Key Concepts <text x="10" y="25" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#2f4f7f" text-anchor="start" data-narration="Strategic Subgame Perfect Equilibrium (SSPE) is a Nash equilibrium that is also a subgame perfect equilibrium. Best Response is the action that maximizes the payoff for a player, given the actions of the other players. Pareto Optimality is a state where no player can improve their payoff without worsening the payoff of another player."

Conclusion <text x="10" y="25" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#2f4f7f" text-anchor="start" data-narration="Repeated games are a fundamental concept in Algorithmic Game Theory, which studies the strategic interactions between players in a sequential decision-making process. Understanding repeated games is essential for analyzing the behavior of players in various economic and social scenarios."
